topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet swam before my eyes, a grotesque landscape of numbers that refused to align. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared at the figures detailing the quarterly compensation. Then I saw it. A stark, undeniable discrepancy. Liam, the new intern who always seemed to glide through the office with an infuriating air of effortless competence, was earning nearly double what I was making for the exact same workload. A slow, hot pressure began to build behind my sternum. I gripped the mouse so tightly my knuckles turned white. Every calculated move I made felt tainted by this glaring imbalance. I wanted to shout, to slam my fist onto the desk, but I kept my mouth shut, forcing a rigid stillness that felt brittle.

[story 2]

"Are you quite finished with that report, Sarah?" Mark’s voice was casual, entirely devoid of the tension I was currently radiating. I forced a smile, a thin, stretched thing that didn't reach my eyes. I glanced over at Chloe, who was laughing easily with David, and then back to the screen displaying the payroll summary. The numbers confirmed my suspicion—Chloe, who had been here six months, was pulling in significantly more. A sudden, sharp clenching in my jaw made my temples throb. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the polished floor. My hands were shaking slightly as I walked toward the water cooler, needing a physical outlet for the churning feeling inside. I took a long, deliberate gulp of water, trying to steady the rapid pulse hammering in my neck.

[story 3]

It was a quiet Tuesday afternoon, the kind where the fluorescent lights hummed with oppressive monotony. I was reviewing expense reports when the reality hit me like a physical blow. Ben, the junior analyst, had just received his salary confirmation, and the figure was staggering compared to mine. A cold, hard knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward into a restless energy. I picked up my pen, but the plastic felt alien in my hand. I began tapping it rapidly against the desk, a frantic, uneven rhythm. My breathing became shallow and quick. I couldn't focus on the data anymore; all I could see was the gulf between my own pay slip and his. I pushed my chair back, the sound echoing too loudly in the empty cubicle, and began pacing the small space, my gaze fixed on the wall as if searching for an answer that simply wasn't there.
